content:
# Numbers Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 172-173 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0127-0128
**Source Images:** `NUM_020_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_020_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the Sons of Israel -- the entire congregation -- arrived at the wilderness of Zin in the first month. The people settled at Kadesh. And Miriam died there and was buried there.

**2** And there was no water for the congregation, so they gathered together against Moses and against Aaron.

**3** And the people quarreled with Moses and said, "If only we had perished when our brothers perished before YHWH!

**4** And why have you brought the assembly of YHWH into this wilderness -- for us and our livestock to die here?

**5** And why did you bring us up out of Egypt to bring us to this terrible place? It is no place for grain, or figs, or vines, or pomegranates -- and there is no water to drink!"

**6** And Moses and Aaron went from the presence of the assembly to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting and fell on their faces. And the glory of YHWH appeared to them.

**7** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**8** "Take the staff and assemble the congregation -- you and Aaron your brother -- and speak to the rock before their eyes, and it will yield its water. You will bring water out of the rock for them and give the congregation and their livestock something to drink."

**9** And Moses took the staff from before YHWH, just as he had commanded him.

**10** And Moses and Aaron assembled the congregation in front of the rock. And he said to them, "Listen now, you rebels! Must we bring water out of this rock for you?"

**11** And Moses raised his hand and struck the rock with his staff two times. And water came gushing out abundantly, and the congregation and their livestock drank.

**12** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron, "Because you did not trust in me -- to treat me as holy before the eyes of the Sons of Israel -- therefore you will not bring this assembly into the land that I have given them."

**13** These are the waters of Meribah, where the Sons of Israel quarreled with YHWH, and he showed himself holy among them.

**14** And Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom: "This is what your brother Israel says: You know all the hardship that has found us --

**15** how our fathers went down to Egypt, and we lived in Egypt a long time, and the Egyptians treated us badly -- us and our fathers.

**16** And we cried out to YHWH, and he heard our voice, and he sent a messenger and brought us out of Egypt. And here we are at Kadesh, a town on the edge of your territory.

**17** Please let us pass through your land. We will not go through any field or vineyard, and we will not drink water from any well. We will travel along the King's Highway -- we will not turn to the right or to the left -- until we have passed through your territory."

**18** And Edom said to him, "You will not pass through my land, or I will come out against you with the sword."

**19** And the Sons of Israel said to him, "We will go up by the highway, and if we drink any of your water -- I or my livestock -- I will pay the price for it. It is nothing -- just let me pass through on foot."

**20** And he said, "You will not pass through." And Edom came out against him with a large fighting force and a strong hand.

**21** And Edom refused to allow Israel to pass through his territory. And Israel turned away from him.

**22** And they set out from Kadesh. And the Sons of Israel -- the entire congregation -- came to Mount Hor.

**23** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron at Mount Hor, on the border of the land of Edom, saying:

**24** "Aaron is to be gathered to his people, because he will not enter the land that I have given to the Sons of Israel, since you both rebelled against my command at the waters of Meribah.

**25** Take Aaron and Eleazar his son and bring them up to Mount Hor.

**26** And strip Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron will be gathered to his people and will die there."

**27** And Moses did just as YHWH had commanded. And they went up Mount Hor before the eyes of the entire congregation.

**28** And Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron died there on the top of the mountain. And Moses and Eleazar came down from the mountain.

**29** And the entire congregation saw that Aaron had died. And all the house of Israel mourned for Aaron thirty days.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 -- Miriam's death and the silence surrounding it** (Heb. *va-yavo'u venei Yisrael kol ha-edah midbar Tzin ba-chodesh ha-rishon va-yeshev ha-am be-Qadesh va-tamat sham Miryam va-tiqqaver sham*): The chapter opens with a devastating compression. Miriam's death is recorded in a single clause -- *va-tamat sham Miryam va-tiqqaver sham* ("And Miriam died there and was buried there") -- without eulogy, without mourning period, without any of the public grief that will attend Aaron's death at the chapter's end (v. 29, "all the house of Israel mourned for Aaron thirty days"). The double *sham* ("there") emphasizes the place: Kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin, the same region from which the spies were sent out a generation earlier (Num 13:21, 26). Miriam dies where Israel's faith failed. The Talmud (Ta'anit 9a) connects the immediate juxtaposition of Miriam's death (v. 1) and the water crisis (v. 2) with the tradition of "Miriam's well" -- a miraculous source of water that accompanied Israel through the wilderness in Miriam's merit, which ceased when she died. The connection is narratively compelling: *ve-lo hayah mayim la-edah* ("and there was no water for the congregation") follows Miriam's burial with no transition, as though the water vanished the moment she was placed in the ground. The Leningrad Codex on page 172 shows the open parashah break (petuchah) marking the beginning of chapter 20, clearly visible in the right-hand column. The phrase *ba-chodesh ha-rishon* ("in the first month") is given without specifying the year -- the only calendar notice in the wilderness narrative that omits the year. This chronological gap has been the subject of much discussion: most traditional interpreters understand it as the first month of the fortieth year (connecting it to the notice of Aaron's death in the fifth month of the fortieth year at Num 33:38), meaning that the thirty-eight years of wilderness wandering -- the silent generation -- pass between the end of Numbers 19 and the beginning of Numbers 20. The text leaps from the year of departure to the year of arrival at the border, treating the intervening decades as a narratively empty period. The Vaticanus (pa_0127, second column) reads *kai ēlthen... eis tēn erēmon Sin en tō mēni tō prōtō* ("and came into the desert of Sin in the first month"), preserving the Hebrew exactly.

**v. 2-5 -- The complaint and the echoes of Exodus 17** (Heb. *ve-lo hayah mayim la-edah va-yiqqahalu al Mosheh ve-al Aharon... va-yarev ha-am im Mosheh*): The water crisis of Numbers 20 deliberately echoes the water crisis of Exodus 17:1-7 (Massah and Meribah), creating a literary bracket around the wilderness period. Both episodes feature: (1) no water, (2) the people quarreling (*riv*) with Moses, (3) a complaint that they were brought out of Egypt to die, (4) water produced from a rock, and (5) a place-name derived from the root *riv* ("to quarrel, contend"). The verb *va-yarev* (v. 3) is the same root that will name the location *Meribah* in verse 13. But there are crucial differences from Exodus 17. In the earlier episode, YHWH instructs Moses to strike the rock (*ve-hikkita va-tzur*, Exod 17:6); here, he is told to speak to it (*ve-dibartem el ha-sela*, v. 8). In Exodus 17, there is no sin; here, Moses' action at the rock will cost him entry to the Promised Land. The complaint formula *lu gava'nu* ("if only we had perished," v. 3) uses the same unusual verb *gava* that appears in Numbers 17:27-28, where the terrified Israelites cry out after Korah's rebellion: "We are perishing! We are dying! All of us are dying!" The echo ties these complaints together as part of a pattern: the wilderness generation cannot stop wishing for death even as YHWH sustains them. The catalog of absent produce in verse 5 -- *lo meqom zera u-te'enah ve-gefen ve-rimmon* ("no place for grain, or figs, or vines, or pomegranates") -- is a bitter anti-description of the Promised Land, since Deuteronomy 8:8 describes that land precisely as a place of wheat, barley, vines, figs, and pomegranates. The people list exactly what they know awaits them, accusing Moses of keeping them from it. The Vaticanus renders *lu apethanomen* ("if only we had died"), using a simple aorist where the Hebrew uses the more emotionally charged *gava*.

**v. 6-8 -- The divine instruction** (Heb. *va-yavo Mosheh ve-Aharon mi-penei ha-qahal el petach ohel mo'ed va-yippelu al peneihem va-yera kevod YHWH aleihem*): Moses and Aaron's response to the crisis follows the established pattern: they withdraw to the Tent of Meeting and prostrate themselves, and the glory of YHWH appears. The phrase *va-yera kevod YHWH* ("and the glory of YHWH appeared") marks this as a moment of divine intervention paralleling Numbers 14:10, 16:19, and 16:42 -- each time the glory appears in response to communal crisis. YHWH's instruction is precise: *qach et ha-matteh... ve-dibartem el ha-sela le-eineihem ve-natan meimav* ("Take the staff... and speak to the rock before their eyes, and it will yield its water"). The verb *dibartem* ("you [plural] shall speak") is second person masculine plural, addressed to both Moses and Aaron. The rock (*sela*) is to respond to speech, not to force. The staff (*matteh*) is to be taken -- but not used to strike. Its presence serves as a visible symbol of divine authority, a reminder of all the signs performed with it, but the operative action is to be verbal: speak, and the rock will give. The Vaticanus reads *kai lalēsete pros tēn petran* ("and you shall speak to the rock"), using *lalēsete* (second plural future) -- confirming the address to both brothers. The Leningrad Codex on page 172 presents these verses in the central column with clear vocalization.

**v. 10-12 -- The sin of Moses and the waters of Meribah** (Heb. *shim'u na ha-morim ha-min ha-sela ha-zeh notzi lakhem mayim... va-yarem Mosheh et yado va-yakh et ha-sela be-matteh-hu pa'amayim*): This is one of the most debated passages in the entire Torah. Moses addresses the people as *ha-morim* ("rebels" -- from the root *marah*, "to rebel, be contentious"), then asks the rhetorical question *ha-min ha-sela ha-zeh notzi lakhem mayim* ("Must we bring water out of this rock for you?"), then raises his hand and strikes the rock twice. Water flows abundantly -- *va-yetze'u mayim rabbim* ("and abundant waters came out") -- yet YHWH immediately pronounces judgment: *ya'an lo he'emantem bi le-haqdisheni le-einei benei Yisrael* ("Because you did not trust in me, to treat me as holy before the eyes of the Sons of Israel"). The exact nature of Moses' sin has generated an extraordinary range of interpretations. The main proposals include: (1) **Striking instead of speaking** (Rashi, following Midrash): Moses was told to speak to the rock but struck it, substituting physical force for faith in the power of the word. Had he spoken and the rock obeyed, the people would have reasoned: if a rock, which neither speaks nor hears, obeys YHWH's word, how much more should we? (2) **The double strike** (some medieval commentators): the first strike produced nothing or only a trickle, revealing Moses' doubt; the second strike brought the full flow, but the hesitation between them showed lack of faith. (3) **The pronoun "we"** (Rambam, *Shemoneh Peraqim* ch. 4): Moses said *notzi* ("shall **we** bring out"), attributing the miracle to himself and Aaron rather than to YHWH, thus failing to sanctify God before the people. (4) **The anger** (Rambam, ibid.): Moses displayed inappropriate anger (*shim'u na ha-morim*, "Listen, rebels!"), and for a leader of Moses' stature to lose composure before the people was a desecration of God's name. (5) **The combination** (Abravanel and others): no single act constitutes the sin; rather, the entire sequence -- anger, self-aggrandizement, disobedience in method -- represents a failure of prophetic composure and trust at a critical public moment. YHWH's verdict uses two key terms: *lo he'emantem* ("you did not trust") -- from the root *aleph-mem-nun*, the root of *emunah* ("faith, faithfulness") and *amen* -- and *le-haqdisheni* ("to treat me as holy") -- from *qadash*, "to sanctify." The failure is simultaneously one of faith and one of sanctification: Moses did not trust that speech alone would suffice, and in failing to trust, he failed to demonstrate YHWH's holiness before the people. The Vaticanus reads *ouk episteusate hagiasai me* ("you did not believe so as to sanctify me"), collapsing the two Hebrew infinitival phrases into a compact Greek construction. The Leningrad Codex shows these critical verses clearly on page 172, right column continuing to center column.

**v. 13 -- Meribah and the sanctification of YHWH** (Heb. *hemah mei Merivah asher ravu venei Yisrael et YHWH va-yiqqadesh bam*): The naming etiology connects the place to the verb *rav* ("to quarrel, contend"). But the final clause -- *va-yiqqadesh bam* ("and he showed himself holy among them") -- is startling: despite the failure of Moses and Aaron, YHWH is nonetheless sanctified. The water flows; the people drink; YHWH's provision is manifest even through imperfect human mediation. The passive/reflexive *va-yiqqadesh* ("he was sanctified / he sanctified himself") can be read both ways: YHWH was sanctified by the event (despite Moses' failure) or YHWH sanctified himself through the event (by pronouncing judgment on Moses, thereby demonstrating that he holds even his most faithful servant accountable). The Vaticanus reads *kai hēgiasthē en autois* ("and he was sanctified among them").

**v. 14-17 -- The appeal to Edom** (Heb. *va-yishlach Mosheh mal'akhim mi-Qadesh el melekh Edom koh amar achicha Yisrael*): Moses' diplomatic message to Edom is a masterwork of rhetorical strategy. He opens with kinship language -- *achicha Yisrael* ("your brother Israel") -- invoking the Genesis narrative of Jacob and Esau (Gen 25-33), where Israel (Jacob) and Edom (Esau) are twin brothers. The appeal to brotherhood is meant to activate familial obligation. The message then provides a compressed history: descent to Egypt, long sojourn, oppression, cry to YHWH, divine deliverance. The phrase *va-yishlach mal'akh* ("and he sent a messenger/angel," v. 16) is ambiguous -- *mal'akh* can mean either "messenger" or "angel" -- and may refer to the angel of YHWH at the burning bush (Exod 3:2) or to Moses himself as YHWH's emissary. The request is framed with maximum deference: *na'averah na ve-artzekha* ("please, let us pass through your land"), using the particle *na* of entreaty. The promises that follow -- no fields, no vineyards, no well-water, strict adherence to the *derekh ha-melekh* ("the King's Highway") -- are designed to remove every conceivable objection. The King's Highway (*derekh ha-melekh*) was a major north-south trade route running through Transjordan from the Gulf of Aqaba to Damascus, attested in extra-biblical sources. The Vaticanus renders it as *hodos hē basilikē* ("the royal road").

**v. 18-21 -- Edom's refusal** (Heb. *lo ta'avor bi pen ba-cherev etze liqratekha... va-yema'en Edom neton et Yisrael avor bi-gvulo va-yet Yisrael me-alav*): Edom's refusal is blunt and threatening: *lo ta'avor bi* ("you shall not pass through my territory") backed by the threat of armed force (*pen ba-cherev*, "lest I come out with the sword"). Israel's second appeal (v. 19) adds the offer to pay for any water consumed -- *ve-im meimekha nishteh ani u-miqnai ve-natatti mikhram* ("and if I drink any of your water -- I and my livestock -- I will pay their price") -- reducing the request to its most minimal form. The phrase *raq ein davar* ("it is nothing" / "it is only a small thing") emphasizes the triviality of the request. But Edom comes out *be-am kaved u-ve-yad chazaqah* ("with a heavy/numerous people and with a strong hand"). The phrase *yad chazaqah* ("strong hand") is loaded with irony: it is YHWH's signature phrase for the Exodus (*be-yad chazaqah*, Exod 6:1, 13:9, Deut 4:34, 5:15, 6:21, etc.) -- Edom now mimics the gesture of Pharaoh, opposing Israel's passage with the same language that described their oppressor. Israel's response -- *va-yet Yisrael me-alav* ("and Israel turned away from him") -- is strikingly non-violent. Unlike the later encounter with Sihon king of Heshbon (Num 21:21-25), where refusal leads to war and conquest, Israel simply turns aside from Edom. The fraternal relationship constrains military action: you do not make war on your brother, even when your brother acts like Pharaoh. This restraint is later codified in Deuteronomy 2:4-6 and 23:8 ("You shall not abhor an Edomite, for he is your brother"). The Vaticanus reads *kai exeklinen Israēl ap' autou* ("and Israel turned aside from him").

**v. 22-29 -- The death of Aaron on Mount Hor** (Heb. *va-yis'u mi-Qadesh va-yavo'u venei Yisrael kol ha-edah Hor ha-Har*): The chapter closes with the death of Aaron, forming an inclusio with Miriam's death in verse 1 -- the chapter is bracketed by the deaths of Moses' two siblings, leaving him alone to face the final stretch of the wilderness journey. Mount Hor (*Hor ha-Har*, literally "Hor the mountain" or "the mountain of the mountain" -- the doubling may indicate a prominent peak, or *Hor* may be a proper name of unknown etymology) is located *al gevul eretz Edom* ("on the border of the land of Edom"), meaning Aaron dies in sight of the territory that has just refused them passage. YHWH's announcement of Aaron's death (vv. 24-26) uses the euphemism *ye'asef el ammav* ("he will be gathered to his people") -- the same phrase used for Abraham (Gen 25:8), Ishmael (Gen 25:17), Isaac (Gen 35:29), and Jacob (Gen 49:33). It implies reunion with ancestors rather than mere cessation of life. But YHWH also provides the reason: *al asher meritem et pi* ("because you [plural] rebelled against my command") at the waters of Meribah. The verb *meritem* ("you rebelled") is from *marah* ("to rebel"), the same root as *ha-morim* ("rebels") that Moses hurled at the people in verse 10 -- a devastating irony. Moses called the people rebels; YHWH now calls Moses and Aaron rebels. The priestly succession is enacted on the mountaintop: *ve-hafshet et Aharon et begadav ve-hilbashtem et El'azar beno* ("strip Aaron of his garments and clothe Eleazar his son in them"). The garments (*begadim*) are the priestly vestments -- the physical embodiment of the office. Their transfer is a visible, tactile act: one man is unclothed, another is clothed; one generation ends, another begins. Aaron dies *be-rosh ha-har* ("on the top of the mountain"), stripped of his priestly identity, returned to the bare humanity with which he began. The Vaticanus reads *kai exedusen Mōusēs ton Aarōn ta himatia autou kai enedusen auta Eleazar* ("and Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and clothed Eleazar in them"), using *exeduō/enduō* (to unclothe/to clothe), a pair that emphasizes the symmetry of the transfer.

**v. 29 -- The mourning for Aaron** (Heb. *va-yir'u kol ha-edah ki gava Aharon va-yivku et Aharon sheloshim yom kol beit Yisrael*): The verb *gava* ("he expired, died") -- the same verb the people used in their death-wish in verse 3 (*lu gava'nu*, "if only we had died") -- now describes Aaron's actual death. The people wished for death; Aaron receives it. The mourning period is thirty days, and the mourners are *kol beit Yisrael* ("all the house of Israel") -- a phrase that contrasts with the mourning for Moses in Deuteronomy 34:8, where the mourners are *benei Yisrael* ("the Sons of Israel"). The Talmud (Mo'ed Qatan 28b) notes this distinction: Aaron was mourned by "all the house of Israel" -- men and women alike -- because he was a pursuer of peace (*rodef shalom*) who reconciled quarreling parties. The chapter ends in grief. It began with the silent burial of Miriam, moved through the water crisis and Moses' fateful act, passed through the diplomatic humiliation by Edom, and culminates in the public death of the high priest on a mountaintop. All three of the Exodus generation's leaders -- Miriam, Aaron, and Moses -- receive their death sentences or die in this single chapter. The wilderness generation is ending. The Leningrad Codex presents these final verses clearly on page 173.

**The architecture of Numbers 20**: The chapter is structured as a chiasm of loss. At the center stands the sin at Meribah (vv. 7-13), which determines the fate of both Moses and Aaron. Flanking this are the two deaths that frame the chapter: Miriam at the beginning (v. 1), Aaron at the end (vv. 22-29). The Edom episode (vv. 14-21) serves as a hinge between the central sin and the final death, introducing the theme of blocked passage -- Israel cannot enter Edom's territory just as Moses and Aaron cannot enter the Promised Land. The chapter is thus a meditation on exclusion: exclusion from water, exclusion from the land of Edom, exclusion from the Promised Land, exclusion from life itself. And yet within this structure of loss, YHWH provides: the water flows (v. 11), the priesthood continues (v. 28), the people move on (v. 22). The God who excludes also sustains. The chapter refuses to let either truth -- judgment or provision -- cancel the other.
